synthesis:
# What This Means for Human Dignity & Labor

Across governance, faith, education, and employment, institutions are outsourcing core human functions to AI before establishing whether those functions can be meaningfully automated without destroying their legitimacy. CAISI's MOU with GSA embeds AI evaluation into federal procurement workflows rather than legislative oversight, while Nvidia unilaterally defines "policy enforcement" for autonomous agents before governments establish baseline legal standards—both moves that treat technical systems as self-legitimating rather than requiring human authorization. Meanwhile, the U.S. labor market's exposure analysis shows high-income knowledge work faces greater replacement risk than manual labor, and Salesforce's former AI CEO confirms the damage is shifting from unemployment to structural wage depression, meaning workers will retain hollow versions of jobs stripped of their economic and status value.

This accelerates the decoupling of work from dignity. Watch whether writing faculty at major universities succeed in establishing explicit refusal rights to AI-assisted assessment—that decision will signal whether institutions view human judgment as irreplaceable to credentialing, or whether they've accepted that machines can certify thinking they cannot themselves perform.
